project.name: hippo
project.description: Hippo is a local-first knowledge capture daemon for macOS: a Rust daemon captures shell, Claude Code, and Firefox browsing activity, redacts secrets, and stores events in SQLite, while a Python "brain" enriches them with a local OpenAI-compatible LLM and serves a searchable second brain over an MCP server — with no data leaving the machine. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the top-level README to understand what hippo is: a local-first second brain for macOS. It lays out the core data flow you will follow for the rest of this tour: a zsh shell hook fires events over a Unix socket to a Rust daemon, which redacts secrets and writes SQLite (WAL); a Python brain then enriches those events with a local LLM into knowledge nodes plus sqlite-vec embeddings, served back via CLI, an HTTP server, and an MCP server. Keep this mental model handy; every later step is one box in that pipeline.

tour.title: Daemon Entry Point
tour.description: main.rs is the binary that everything runs as: it parses CLI arguments and dispatches either into the long-lived daemon run loop or into one of the many subcommands (doctor, probe, watchdog, codex-poll, native-messaging-host). Reading it gives you the full surface area of the Rust side in one place. The daemon's lib.rs declares the module layout and has the highest fan-out in the codebase, so it is the map of where each capability lives.
tour.languageLesson: Rust separates a crate's library (lib.rs) from its binary (main.rs). main.rs stays thin and delegates into the library so the same logic is reachable from both the binary and integration tests.

tour.title: The Capture Path: Socket and Daemon Loop
tour.description: This is the heart of capture. The shell hook is a fire-and-forget client that writes length-prefixed JSON to a Unix socket; daemon.rs is the server side, accepting connections, dispatching requests, and flushing captured events to storage. Building on the entry point from Step 2, this is the loop that actually receives every shell command before it ever touches the database. The hook deliberately does no heavy work so it never blocks your shell.
tour.languageLesson: A Unix domain socket with length-prefixed framing lets the shell hand off an event and exit immediately (fire-and-forget), keeping the interactive shell fast while the daemon does the durable write asynchronously.

tour.title: Shared Core Library
tour.description: hippo-core is the shared Rust crate that both the daemon and its tests depend on; its lib.rs re-exports the modules that define hippo's vocabulary. protocol.rs defines the wire format the socket speaks, events.rs defines the captured-event types, and config.rs loads the runtime config that gates every behavior. These are the types the daemon in Steps 2-3 was moving around, now seen from the inside.

tour.title: Storage Engine and Schema
tour.description: storage.rs is the SQLite engine: it opens connections in WAL mode with foreign keys on and a busy_timeout, and it owns the schema migrations. schema.sql is the canonical data model, defining 30+ tables spanning capture (events, sessions), enrichment queues, knowledge nodes, browser and agentic sources, and the source_health and capture_alarms reliability tables. The schema reference doc walks the per-version migration history. This is where every captured event in Step 3 ultimately lands and where the brain will read from later.
tour.languageLesson: SQLite WAL (write-ahead logging) mode lets the Rust daemon write while the Python brain reads concurrently against the same file, with busy_timeout absorbing brief lock contention instead of erroring out.

tour.title: Secret Redaction (Privacy-First)
tour.description: Redaction is hippo's defining privacy guarantee: every captured event is scrubbed for secrets before it is ever written to disk. redaction.rs is the pattern engine, redact.default.toml ships the default secret patterns (API keys, tokens, env vars), and the redaction reference documents the pattern format and threat model. Because this runs inside the capture path from Step 3, secrets never reach the storage layer from Step 5 in the first place.

tour.title: Agentic and Browser Source Ingestion
tour.description: Beyond the shell, hippo ingests AI coding sessions and browsing. watch_claude_sessions.rs is a long-lived FSEvents watcher that re-parses Claude Code JSONL transcripts into idempotent segments, codex_session.rs polls Codex rollout files into the same shared enrichment queue, and native_messaging.rs is the bridge that receives browser visits from the Firefox extension's background script. All three normalize into the same tables from Step 5, so the brain enriches them through one path.

tour.title: The Brain: Enrichment Pipeline
tour.description: Now cross into Python. The brain polls enrichment queues from SQLite and calls a local OpenAI-compatible LLM to turn raw events into structured knowledge nodes. enrichment.py is the most depended-upon module in the brain (highest fan-in), orchestrating that pipeline; client.py is the vendor-neutral inference client it calls. Shell, Claude, and browser sources are enriched concurrently via asyncio.gather so LLM latency overlaps across sources.
tour.languageLesson: asyncio.gather runs multiple enrichment coroutines concurrently on one thread; while one source waits on the LLM's network call, others make progress, so total wall-clock time approaches the slowest single call rather than the sum.

tour.title: Embeddings and Retrieval
tour.description: Enriched knowledge needs to be searchable. embeddings.py generates vectors for each knowledge node and vector_store.py writes them into sqlite-vec (vec0) virtual tables alongside an FTS5 full-text index. retrieval.py implements hybrid search, combining semantic vector similarity with lexical FTS matches over the nodes produced in Step 8. Embeddings run as background tasks so they overlap with ongoing LLM inference rather than serializing behind it.

tour.title: Query Surface: RAG, HTTP Server, and MCP
tour.description: This is where knowledge flows back out. rag.py embeds a question, retrieves relevant nodes via the hybrid search from Step 9, and synthesizes an answer with source references; server.py exposes the brain's HTTP query endpoint (used by non-raw hippo commands); and mcp.py is the MCP server that lets Claude Code call ask, search_knowledge, search_events, and get_entities directly. Together they close the loop the README opened in Step 1.

tour.title: Capture Reliability: Watchdog, Probe, source_health
tour.description: A capture system is only trustworthy if you know it is actually capturing. The source_health table is the single SQL ground truth of whether each source's events are landing; watchdog.rs asserts the I-1..I-14 invariants against it every 60 seconds and writes capture_alarms rows on violations; probe.rs round-trips synthetic canary events through every capture path to measure latency and detect silent failures. The architecture doc ties these four layers together. This is the reliability stack the README promises.

tour.title: Operations: launchd, Observability, and CI
tour.description: Finally, how it all runs and stays healthy in production-on-a-laptop. The launchd plists keep the five core services alive (daemon, brain, claude-session-watcher, watchdog, probe) with KeepAlive and OTel wiring; the OTel docker-compose stack provides Grafana/Loki/Tempo/Prometheus for local telemetry; and the Rust and Python GitHub Actions workflows gate every change with lint, test, and clippy. config.default.toml is the single runtime config that tunes models, enrichment, retrieval, and the watchdog across all of them.
tour.languageLesson: launchd LaunchAgents with KeepAlive=true restart a crashed process automatically; RunAtLoad starts it at login, and StartInterval drives the periodic poll-style jobs (probe, watchdog) on a fixed cadence.
